title: Whistleblower

jokes.text: Michael Scott, as seen on TV. I saw you on the news and I want to pinch your tiny wiener.
jokes.setup: Packer calling into the office after seeing Michael on the news
jokes.punchline: Inappropriate sexual comment delivered in Packer's signature crude style
jokes.explanation: Pure Packer - only he would make this call. Character-specific but relies on crude shock value.

dialogue.speaker: Packer
dialogue.line: Hey, I just saw you on the news. You looked great. I would've done you right there on camera.

jokes.text: Oh! Pac-Man, I thought you were a girl.
jokes.setup: Michael's confusion about who's calling
jokes.punchline: His innocent misunderstanding of Packer's voice
jokes.explanation: Michael's obliviousness perfectly captured in one line. Good economy and character work.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Hello?

dialogue.speaker: Packer
dialogue.line: Hey Michael, it's me.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Who is this?

dialogue.speaker: Packer
dialogue.line: It's Packer, man.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Oh my God, I thought you were a woman on the phone. Your voice is so high and feminine.

jokes.text: What's number one? Oh? That teacher who was wrongfully accused of being a pedophile. Now, we cannot let the pedophile win again.
jokes.setup: Michael wanting his clip to be most watched on news site
jokes.punchline: His completely inappropriate competitive framing with a wrongly accused teacher
jokes.explanation: Dark and inappropriate in a way only Michael could deliver. The phrase 'cannot let the pedophile win again' is both shocking and perfectly character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I want my clip to be the most watched on that news site.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I'm going up against a teacher who was wrongly accused of something inappropriate with a student. How is that fair? She's got sympathy!

jokes.text: Get it up. That's what... Let's do it.
jokes.setup: Michael telling people to get the website up
jokes.punchline: His accidental sexual innuendo and immediate awareness of it
jokes.explanation: Standard 'That's what she said' setup but Michael catches himself mid-sentence, which is character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I need this website up and running. I want it hard, I want it fast, I want it deep...

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: That's what she said. No, wait...

jokes.text: Really? A baby otter? Okay, count me in as 'Who cares?'
jokes.setup: Office workers distracted by cute baby otter video
jokes.punchline: Michael's dismissive response showing his self-centeredness
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael selfishness - can't stand not being the center of attention. Good economy in the phrasing.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Cute baby otter? I don't have time for that. I'm too busy being the World's Best Boss.

jokes.text: It can't even stand up. It's trying to stand up. There he goes.
jokes.setup: Michael claiming the baby otter isn't interesting
jokes.punchline: Him getting immediately invested and narrating its movements
jokes.explanation: Beautiful character contradiction - Michael can't help but get invested despite his dismissal. The progression from dismissive to engaged is perfectly executed.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: A baby otter? That's not interesting.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Oh my God, look at it. Look at how it's moving through the water. So graceful. And now it's turning. It's turning again. Why is it turning so much? Is it looking for food? I think it's looking for food.

jokes.text: Jo, I think that I know what happened. I'm not sure you do, teddy bear. Well, now I think I might not.
jokes.setup: Michael confidently claiming he knows what happened
jokes.punchline: His immediate backdown when Jo calls him 'teddy bear'
jokes.explanation: Michael's instant capitulation to authority figure calling him a pet name is perfectly in character. Great economy.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I know exactly what happened here. I've got this completely figured out.

dialogue.speaker: Jo
dialogue.line: Oh teddy bear, you have no idea what you're talking about, do you?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: No. No, I don't. You're right. I was wrong about all of it.

jokes.text: Put your hand up, Norma Rae. If you say anything, so help me God, I'll break off the temples of your glasses and stick them in your eye sockets.
jokes.setup: Jo asking whoever talked to press to raise their hand
jokes.punchline: Dwight's oddly specific and violent threat
jokes.explanation: Pure Dwight - the Norma Rae reference combined with the bizarrely specific violence is quintessential character comedy. Only Dwight would threaten eye socket violence with glasses temples.

dialogue.speaker: Jo
dialogue.line: Whoever talked to the press, raise your hand.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: If I find out who did this, I will decapitate you with a chainsaw and drink your blood from a boot.

jokes.text: The Bernards, for generations, have silenced whistleblowers. It's how we made all our money. Woody Guthrie wrote a song about us. Old Mister Bernard, Who have you silenced today?
jokes.setup: Dwight explaining why he wouldn't be a whistleblower
jokes.punchline: His ridiculous family history with a fake Woody Guthrie song
jokes.explanation: Classic Dwight fabrication with perfect details - the fake Woody Guthrie song with lyrics is inspired absurdity that feels completely authentic to his character.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I would never be a whistleblower. My family has a proud history of not reporting things.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: My great-grandfather was at Kitty Hawk. He saw the Wright brothers fly, and you know what he did? He didn't report it. He just went home and milked his cows.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: There's actually a song about it. *clears throat* 'I ain't gonna report you, no I ain't gonna report you, 'cause my family don't report nobody, we just mind our business and milk our cows.' That's a Woody Guthrie song.

jokes.text: When Mama was working as a prison guard, and something went missing, she'd ask one question, 'What do we do when we find the guilty party?' And if they said, 'Come down on them with that swift hammer of justice,' innocent. A clear conscience don't need no mercy. But if they said, 'Officer Bessie, well, they may have had a reason.' Blah, blah, blah, blah... Well, nine times out of 10, that's the anus they checked.
jokes.setup: Jo explaining her mother's prison guard interrogation technique
jokes.punchline: The crude revelation about 'checking anuses' delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Jo's folksy storytelling building to a crude punchline is perfect character work. The matter-of-fact delivery makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Jo
dialogue.line: My mother was a prison guard. She had this interrogation technique where she would just stare at you. Didn't say anything, just stare. It was very effective.

dialogue.speaker: Jo
dialogue.line: One time, an inmate broke out. She caught him by checking anuses.

jokes.text: We should give them a one-way ticket to Montego Bay. Where they keep all the al-Qaeda. Uh, that's Guantanamo Bay. Yes.
jokes.setup: Jo asking what to do with the whistleblower
jokes.punchline: Michael's geographical confusion mixing up vacation spot with detention center
jokes.explanation: Michael's ignorance played for laughs - confusing tropical vacation with military prison is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Jo
dialogue.line: What are we going to do about this whistleblower?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Well, I say we send them to Sandals Jamaica. It's a beautiful resort. Great food, great drinks, topless beaches.

dialogue.speaker: Jo
dialogue.line: Michael, that's not a detention center.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Oh. Well then, scratch that. How about Guantanamo?

jokes.text: Thanks a lot, Big Brother. You're with Big Brother?
jokes.setup: Andy sarcastically referencing Big Brother about computer searches
jokes.punchline: Dwight taking it literally and asking if Andy's affiliated
jokes.explanation: Dwight's literal interpretation of metaphors is classic character work. Perfect economy in the exchange.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Big Brother is watching our computer searches.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Are you affiliated with Big Brother?

jokes.text: I already got to yours, Kev. No, that's cool. Sometimes I run. I'm a runner.
jokes.setup: Dwight mentioning he searched Kevin's computer
jokes.punchline: Kevin's awkward defensive response about running
jokes.explanation: Kevin's awkward attempt to seem active/healthy when caught is mildly amusing but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I searched your computer.

dialogue.speaker: Kevin
dialogue.line: You can't just search my computer! And I was just running... I was running a program.

jokes.text: Are you referring to alchemy?
jokes.setup: Jo telling Dwight to turn money into more money
jokes.punchline: Dwight's literal interpretation involving medieval practices
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly Dwight - his medieval mindset interpreting investment advice as literal alchemy. Perfect economy and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Jo
dialogue.line: Dwight, I need you to turn this money into more money.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I'll get my alchemy equipment. I've been studying medieval transmutation texts for years.

jokes.text: I swear on the graves of my parents who aren't even dead yet.
jokes.setup: Jim trying to convince Jo he didn't leak
jokes.punchline: His illogical oath that immediately undermines itself
jokes.explanation: Jim's overthinking creates a self-contradictory oath. Good structure and economy in the absurd logic.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Jo, I swear on my life, I did not leak those documents. I would never do that.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: And if I'm lying, may I be struck down by lightning... unless it rains that day, because obviously I'm not going outside in a thunderstorm.

jokes.text: I was talking to this girl at a bar. Oh, no, no, no. I think she could sense my sadness. And I found out too late that she was a copyeditor at the Trib.
jokes.setup: Darryl admitting he told someone outside the office
jokes.punchline: His romantic delusion about sharing his sadness with a reporter
jokes.explanation: Darryl's misreading of romantic signals leading to corporate disaster is solidly constructed cringe comedy.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Yeah, I told a reporter about it.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: I thought maybe she'd write about it, and then... I don't know, maybe she'd fall in love with me or something.

jokes.text: This is just a mystery novel that I've been working on. I know what it is. I skimmed the first chapter. I'm just curious, why would a man who hates people want to have a relationship with a maid? I don't know... The way I look at it, there's only one of two reasons. He knows a secret about her that she doesn't know herself. Or he wants to use her services to mop up after a murder.
jokes.setup: Jo finding Jim's novel on his computer
jokes.punchline: Jo's dark and logical analysis of Jim's amateur mystery plot
jokes.explanation: Jo's practical dissection of Jim's creative writing reveals both characters well - his amateur efforts and her no-nonsense analysis.

dialogue.speaker: Jo
dialogue.line: Jim, I read your novel. Your detective protagonist is an alcoholic who solves crimes by accident while blackout drunk.

dialogue.speaker: Jo
dialogue.line: That's not a mystery plot. That's a cry for help disguised as fiction.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: It's a work in progress.

dialogue.speaker: Jo
dialogue.line: The progress stopped at 'incoherent.' Your second chapter is literally just the word 'whiskey' repeated 47 times.

jokes.text: My mind is going a mile an hour. That fast?
jokes.setup: Jim saying his mind is racing under pressure
jokes.punchline: Pam's deadpan pointing out that's actually very slow
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup/punchline structure. Pam's quick wit catches Jim's malapropism with excellent economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: My mind is racing under pressure.

dialogue.speaker: Pam
dialogue.line: That's actually very slow.

jokes.text: I weirdly know exactly what you're saying to me. So, I'll see you in Meredith's van in five minutes. Okay, you didn't need to actually say that.
jokes.setup: Jim and Darryl having a wordless understanding
jokes.punchline: Jim verbalizing what was meant to be unspoken, Darryl calling it out
jokes.explanation: Meta moment about TV communication conventions. Jim ruins the cool unspoken understanding by speaking.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: So we both know what this is about, right?

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Yeah, man. We don't even need to say it.

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Exactly. It's this whole thing with Michael and the—

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Dude. What are you doing?

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: What?

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: You just said it out loud.

jokes.text: It is so boring where we work. I mean, it's as interesting as a morgue. It might be less interesting than a morgue. Hey, hey, it's as interesting as a morgue.
jokes.setup: Kelly explaining why she leaked the story
jokes.punchline: Her escalating comparison to a morgue, then Jim stopping her from going further
jokes.explanation: Kelly's tendency to over-dramatize checked by Jim. The escalation structure works but isn't exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: I had to tell someone! It was like I was a morgue and the secret was a dead body just lying there.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: And then it started to smell, so I had to let it out or I would've exploded like a corpse.

dialogue.speaker: Kelly
dialogue.line: Actually, it's more like I was a morgue that was also a—

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Okay, I think we got it.

jokes.text: I spend the whole day talking. I mean, I video chat, I skype, I text, I tweet, I phone, I woof. Woof is a site that I'm launching to be the last word in social networking. For just $12.99 a month, Woof links up all your communication portals, so you are always within reach. It's part of the dog pack, as I call it.
jokes.setup: Ryan explaining his constant communication
jokes.punchline: His ridiculous 'Woof' social media platform with dog-themed branding
jokes.explanation: Pure Ryan entrepreneurial delusion. The dog theme and corporate speak parody his character perfectly, though it runs a bit long.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: I've been working on this new social media platform called Woof. It's dog-themed. Every post is a bark, you can only communicate in growls, and your profile picture has to be you as a dog.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: It's going to revolutionize communication. I'm thinking of taking it public next quarter.

jokes.text: I just sent myself a woof. Ryan, you have a woof on line one. Thank you, Erin. Woof!
jokes.setup: Ryan demonstrating his Woof platform
jokes.punchline: The ridiculous demonstration with Erin playing along and Ryan barking
jokes.explanation: Physical demonstration of Ryan's absurd business idea. Erin's participation makes it work but it's not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Okay, so with Woof, you can connect with dog lovers in your area. Watch this.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Oh my god, I love dogs!

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: I know you do. Now, I'm going to bark, and you bark back.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Woof woof.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Woof woof woof!

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Woof.

dialogue.speaker: Erin
dialogue.line: Woof woof!

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: See? We're connecting. This is the future of social networking.

jokes.text: All you need is one scapegoat. Uh, if you turn me in, I'm turning in Darryl. That's just what we need, another black man in prison, you know?
jokes.setup: Ryan suggesting they need one scapegoat
jokes.punchline: His inappropriate racial comment about Darryl going to prison
jokes.explanation: Ryan's casual racism disguised as social awareness is in character but relies on shock value over craft.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: We need one scapegoat.

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Someone to take the fall for all of us.

dialogue.speaker: Darryl
dialogue.line: Why are you looking at me?

dialogue.speaker: Ryan
dialogue.line: Well, you know, statistically speaking...

jokes.text: Basement office? You mean like a lair?
jokes.setup: Dwight mentioning the property has a basement office
jokes.punchline: Jo's immediate association with villainous lairs
jokes.explanation: Jo's practical mind immediately going to villainous applications. Good economy and realistic thought process.

dialogue.speaker: Jo
dialogue.line: A basement office? That's where all the villains have their lairs.

jokes.text: Are you gonna kill me?
jokes.setup: Jo taking Michael away from the office for private conversation
jokes.punchline: Michael's paranoid fear delivered matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: Michael's paranoia in the face of authority is character-appropriate. Simple but effective.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: She's taking me away. She's gonna fire me. She's gonna kill me and bury me in the woods.

jokes.text: Make them an offer they can't refuse. No, on second thought, lowball them.
jokes.setup: Dwight making a dramatic Godfather reference about property
jokes.punchline: Immediately undermining the drama with practical cheapness
jokes.explanation: Perfect Dwight - dramatic reference immediately undercut by his cheapness. Good misdirection structure.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I'm going to make him an offer he can't refuse. I'll offer him $0.50 per square foot.

jokes.text: And? You did it. What? Based on what? Just all the evidence. And it really seems like it was you.
jokes.setup: Dwight concluding his investigation
jokes.punchline: His complete lack of actual evidence or logic
jokes.explanation: Dwight's authoritarian tendencies without any actual authority or evidence. His confident nonsense is perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I have concluded my investigation. The culprit is Stanley Hudson.

dialogue.speaker: Stanley
dialogue.line: What? On what evidence?

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I don't need evidence. I have instinct. And a beet farm.

jokes.text: Can we all agree to say that it was Andy for now and sit with it, see how it feels?
jokes.setup: Dwight's baseless conclusion about Stanley
jokes.punchline: His suggestion to just blame Andy as a trial run
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd Dwight logic - treating false accusations like trying on clothes. The phrase 'sit with it, see how it feels' applied to scapegoating is inspired.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Stanley's been acting strange lately. I've narrowed it down to three possibilities: he's either a vampire, a werewolf, or he's been replaced by an identical-looking impostor.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Here's what we do: we blame Andy. It's the perfect trial run. If it works, we know our blaming infrastructure is solid.

jokes.text: My favorite restaurant closed down. Oh. I hate that. And my new favorite restaurant sucks. I bought a video camera last year and I was looking at the tapes, and there were only, like, 12 minutes that I felt was worth taping. The whole year. And most of that was just birds in my condo complex.
jokes.setup: Michael opening up about his rough year
jokes.punchline: His pathetically mundane problems and boring life documentation
jokes.explanation: Michael's sad, mundane problems delivered with complete sincerity create perfect cringe comedy. His boring video documentation is painfully relatable.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: This year has been really rough for me. I mean, my condo association rejected my application to paint my front door periwinkle.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: And then my dentist said I need to floss more. Can you believe that?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: Plus, I've been documenting my entire life in this journal, and honestly, it's just... really boring.

dialogue.speaker: Jo
dialogue.line: Michael, those are the problems of a privileged man.

jokes.text: It has not been a blockbuster year for me financially. My Blockbuster stock is down.
jokes.setup: Michael discussing his financial troubles
jokes.punchline: His unintentional pun about Blockbuster stock being down
jokes.explanation: Michael's accidental wordplay showing his poor investment choices. Good economy and character-appropriate obliviousness.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: My Blockbuster stock is really down. I guess you could say my investment is not rewinding well.

jokes.text: But I'm not here to talk about that. I'm here to talk about Suck It. Suck It... Gabe, I told you all about the printers.
jokes.setup: Packer deflecting from leak discussion
jokes.punchline: His juvenile product name and admission of guilt
jokes.explanation: Pure Packer behavior - juvenile humor and casual admission of corporate sabotage. Character-consistent but not exceptional craft.

dialogue.speaker: Todd Packer
dialogue.line: Well, you know, I've always believed in being honest with my employees. That's why I'm telling you right now: I named my bladder control product 'Packer's Pants Protector' and yes, I may have had a few leaks myself.

jokes.text: Okay, look, I get it, people. I'm the lame IT guy and everybody hates me. Hey, listen, man, you can't take it personally. You call me 'man'? I just said my name, just now. Did you forget it already? No, sport.
jokes.setup: Nick (IT guy) complaining about being ignored
jokes.punchline: Jim immediately proving his point by using generic terms instead of his name
jokes.explanation: Good dramatic irony - Jim immediately proves Nick's point about being ignored. The escalation from 'man' to 'sport' makes it worse.

dialogue.speaker: Nick
dialogue.line: Nobody ever remembers my name. I'm always 'the IT guy.'

dialogue.speaker: Jim
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, the IT guy needs to fix my computer again.

jokes.text: We liked the last guy, Sadiq, because he kept to himself. And we also thought he might have been a terrorist.
jokes.setup: Michael explaining why they preferred the previous IT guy
jokes.punchline: His casual admission of racist profiling
jokes.explanation: Michael's casual racism delivered with complete innocence. Character-appropriate but relies on shock value over craft.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: We had a guy before, and he was great. He really was. But then he left, and we got this new guy, and... I don't know, something's different. The old guy, he just had a way about him. You know what I mean?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: He was just so... competent. And he looked like he knew what he was doing. You could just tell by looking at him.

jokes.text: Inner-city kids use computers for two things, games and porn. So, good luck wasting your life, Lurch.
jokes.setup: Nick responding to dismissive treatment
jokes.punchline: His bitter, inappropriate generalization and insult
jokes.explanation: Nick's bitter lashing out feels authentic to the situation but the humor is mean-spirited and lazy. The Lurch reference dates it.

jokes.text: I saw all of your hard drives. And guess what? You're not a photographer. And you definitely can't fit into a size two. Darryl, man, you're on Facebook. Why have you been telling people you're not on Facebook? People want to be your friend, man.
jokes.setup: Nick revealing embarrassing computer search results
jokes.punchline: His rapid-fire exposure of everyone's digital lies and delusions
jokes.explanation: Nick's revenge reveals universal digital embarrassments. Good escalation structure and feels earned after his mistreatment.

dialogue.speaker: Nick
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, so I pulled up the browser history on the office computer. Jim searched 'how to do a cartwheel' seventeen times. Pam searched 'is my husband cheating' twice. Michael searched 'am I a good boss' forty-three times in one day.

dialogue.speaker: Nick
dialogue.line: Dwight searched 'Chia Pet care tips.' Ryan searched 'how to start a successful company' and then immediately 'how to fake your own death.' And Kevin... Kevin just searched 'bagel recipes' but spelled it wrong every single time.

jokes.text: When I was growing up, there was nothing better than being a big old business tycoon. And I thought I'd break that glass ceiling and be a hero to all those little girls out there. And they'd make a Barbie out of me. I hate that I sell cheap printers. I do. But if I have to go out there in front of the press and make one of them public apology recalls, I mean, that's all I'll ever be remembered for. Nobody will want to play with my Barbie.
jokes.setup: Jo explaining her business ambitions
jokes.punchline: Her childish dream of becoming a Barbie doll
jokes.explanation: Jo's vulnerability revealed through an absurd childhood dream. The Barbie aspiration humanizes her while remaining ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: Jo
dialogue.line: You know what my dream was as a child? To become a Barbie doll.

dialogue.speaker: Jo
dialogue.line: Not to play with Barbie dolls. To actually become one.

jokes.text: Airplane II.
jokes.setup: Michael referencing his 'don't call me honey' line to Jo
jokes.punchline: His proud citation of the movie reference source
jokes.explanation: Peak Michael - citing his pop culture reference with pride. Perfect economy and character integration but relies on reference humor.

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: I said, 'Don't call me honey.' That's a Cher song. From Moonstruck. I'm very cultured.

jokes.text: I own a one-eighth share in a rental property down in Pittston. Well, I'm one-eighths proud of you.
jokes.setup: Dwight bragging about his tiny property investment
jokes.punchline: Jo's mathematical put-down
jokes.explanation: Clever mathematical wordplay that deflates Dwight's bragging perfectly. Jo's quick wit shines through.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: I've invested in 60 acres of prime real estate. It's a sound financial decision.

dialogue.speaker: Jo
dialogue.line: Dwight, 60 acres sounds impressive until you realize you're dividing your net worth by zero.

jokes.text: Like Buckingham Palace.
jokes.setup: Jo telling Dwight he'll be on his feet soon
jokes.punchline: Dwight's random, grandiose comparison
jokes.explanation: Dwight's random royal reference is mildly amusing but doesn't have much structure or payoff.

dialogue.speaker: Jo
dialogue.line: You'll be on your feet soon.

dialogue.speaker: Dwight
dialogue.line: Like a phoenix rising from the ashes. Or a beet rising from the soil.

jokes.text: Look, I didn't want houses and schools to burn down and children to die. Does that make me a hero? No, it does not. Well, it doesn't make me the worst guy in the world, either. It does!
jokes.setup: Andy's self-serving justification for being the whistleblower
jokes.punchline: Angela's immediate, harsh contradiction
jokes.explanation: Good escalation structure with Andy's self-justification immediately shot down by Angela's brutal honesty.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I did it for the company. I'm a hero. I put my neck on the line because I care about this place.

dialogue.speaker: Angela
dialogue.line: You did it because you're an idiot who wanted attention.

jokes.text: Okay. Hey, you could transfer Holly back from Nashua. Let me see what I can do.
jokes.setup: Jo offering to help brighten Michael's life
jokes.punchline: Michael immediately asking for exactly what he wants most
jokes.explanation: Perfect Michael move - immediately asking for the one thing he wants most. Character-consistent but structurally simple.

dialogue.speaker: Jo
dialogue.line: Michael, I'm here to help brighten your life. What do you want most?

dialogue.speaker: Michael
dialogue.line: A shotgun.

insight: Season finale dips to 63.6: character comedy sustains finale despite low-impact setup.
